full_response:
Let the inertial laboratory frame be  
x^μ =(ct, r ,θ ,z) ,   with the Minkowski line element  

ds² = −c²dt² + dr² + r² dθ² + dz² .

To describe the rotating disk we introduce the “rotating” coordinates

t′ = t, 
r′ = r, 
θ′ = θ − ωt, 
z′ = z .                (1)

With these coordinates a point stuck to the rim (r′ = R , θ′ = const) is indeed at rest in the primed system.  
The metric becomes  

ds² = −(1 − ω²r′²/c²)c²dt′² + 2ω r′² dt′ dθ′ + dr′² + r′² dθ′² + dz′² .        (2)

Notice the mixed term 2ω r′² dt′ dθ′ : the surface t′ = const is not orthogonal to the world–lines r′,θ′,z′ = const.  
Orthogonality is the condition for simultaneity in the local rest-frame of the observers:

events are simultaneous for the observer on the rim  ⇔ U_μ dx^μ = 0 ,         (3)

where U^μ is the 4-velocity of that observer.  
For a rim observer (r′ = R, θ′ = const)

U^μ = γ (1, 0, ωR/c , 0)  with γ = 1/√(1−ω²R²/c²) .    (4)

Equation (3) gives the simultaneity condition between two neighbouring points on the rim:

0 = U_μ dx^μ  = γ(−c dt + ωR² dθ /c) ⟹ dt = (ω R² / c²) dθ .        (5)

Thus, if two neighbouring rim observers agree that their measurements are simultaneous, the laboratory time separation between the events must be dt = (ωR²/c²)dθ.

But on a surface t′ = const we have dt = 0 (because t′ = t).  
Comparing with (5) we see that the condition for simultaneity of the rim observers is violated unless dθ = 0. Hence

t′ = constant does not pick out events that are simultaneous for the family of observers riding on the edge of the merry-go-round.

Because simultaneity in a rotating frame is position-dependent, no single global time coordinate can serve all rim observers; to measure the circumference one must instead integrate along the curve that satisfies U_μ dx^μ = 0 at every point, not along t′ = const.

\boxed{\text{Surfaces }t'=\text{const are not simultaneity slices for the riders on the rim because their 4–velocity }U^\mu\text{ is not orthogonal to the normal }dt'_\mu;\; events with the same }t'\text{ occur at different proper times around the rim.}} 

Confidence: 79%
